Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## REVISITING VIRTUAL NODES IN GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR LINK PREDICTION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

It is well known that the graph classification performance of graph neural networks often improves by adding an artificial virtual node to the graphs, which is connected to all nodes in the graph. Intuitively, the virtual node provides a shortcut for message passing between nodes along the graph edges. Surprisingly, the impact of virtual nodes with other problems is still an open research question.

In this paper, we adapt the concept of virtual nodes to the link prediction scenario, where we usually have much larger, often dense, and more heterogeneous graphs. In particular, we use multiple virtual nodes per graph and graph-based clustering to determine the connections to the graph nodes. We also investigate alternative clustering approaches (e.g., random or more advanced) and compare to the original model with a single virtual node. We conducted extensive experiments over different datasets of the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) and analyze the results in detail. We show that our virtual node extensions yield rather stable performance increases and allow standard graph neural networks to compete with complex state-of-the-art models, as well as with the models leading the OGB leaderboards.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

_Link prediction_ is an important task to complete graphs that are missing edges in various domains: citation networks (Kipf & Welling, 2016), social networks (Adamic & Adar, 2003), medical drug interaction graphs (Abbas et al., 2021), or knowledge graphs (KGs) (Ji et al., 2021). Numerous kinds of models have been proposed to solve the link prediction problem, ranging from KG-specific predictors (Ji et al., 2021) to graph neural networks (GNNs) (Kipf & Welling, 2016; Zhang & Chen, 2018). Over dense biomedical networks, GNNs turned out to work especially well (Hu et al., 2020).

In this work, we focus on _graph neural networks_ for link prediction. Many of the popular GNNs are based on the message-passing scheme, which computes node embeddings based on iteratively aggregating the features of (usually direct/one-hop) neighbor nodes along the graph edges (Gilmer et al., 2017). Interestingly, best performance is usually obtained by only considering two to three hops of neighbors (i.e., 2-3 layers in the GNN). One main reason identified for this is _over-smoothing_ , the problem that node representations become indistinguishable when the number of layers increases (Li et al., 2018). The exponentially-growing amount of information has also been suggested as one issue connected to capturing long-range dependencies (Alon & Yahav, 2021). While it is likely that link prediction most often depends on the local node neighborhood, it is not beyond imagination that there are critical long-range dependencies (e.g., complex chains of drug-drug or drug-protein interactions). Hence, using a small number of layers to overcome the above problems results in _under-reaching_ .

There have been several recent proposals to overcome under-reaching. On the one hand, several works propose techniques that allow for larger numbers of GNN layers (Xu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a). However, although (Chen et al., 2020) show that over-smoothing happens particularly in dense graphs, the link prediction experiments in these works consider citation or recommendation networks, but not the especially dense biomedical ones. And our experiments over the latter suggest that the reported results are not generalizable to the more challenging biomedical data. On the other hand, there are approaches that adapt the message-passing scheme to consider neighbors beyond the one-hop neighborhood: based on graph diffusion (Atwood & Towsley, 2016; Klicpera et al., 2019a; Abu-El-Haija et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019a; Ma et al., 2020; Klicpera et al., 2019b) and other theories (Morris et al., 2019; You

et al., 2019). However, most of these models are relatively complex and, in fact, in our experiments over the challenging graphs from the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) (Hu et al., 2020), several ran out of memory. Moreover, the majority has not considered link prediction, while this problem was recently shown to be more difficult than node classification (Zhang et al., 2020).

In this paper, we propose a simple but elegant solution to under-reaching based on the concept of _virtual nodes_ (Gilmer et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Pham et al., 2017; Ishiguro et al., 2019). Virtual nodes are well known to often improve the graph classification performance of graph neural networks, where an artificial virtual node is added to every graph and connected to all nodes in the graph. While the virtual nodes were originally thought as representations of the entire graph, they also provide shortcuts for message passing between nodes along the graph edges. Surprisingly, the impact of virtual nodes for the link prediction problem has not been investigated yet. The reason for this might be that the often very large and heterogeneous “network” graphs in link prediction are of very different nature and require novel/adapted solutions (e.g., a protein interaction network may easily contain millions of nodes, whereas a molecule to be classified contains usually less than fifty).

Figure 1: We cluster graph nodes belonging together (gray shades) and connect them to a common virtual node (blue). This eases information exchange and hence prediction of links between nodes not directly related (red).
We explore application and effects of virtual nodes in link prediction theoretically and empirically:

- _We propose to use multiple virtual nodes in the link prediction scenario and describe a graph-based technique to connect them to the graph nodes._ Consider Figure 1. In a nutshell, we use a graph clustering algorithm to determine groups of nodes in the graph that belong together and then connect these nodes to a common virtual node. In this way, under-reaching is decreased because clustered nodes can share information easily; at the same time, the nodes are spared of unnecessary information from unrelated nodes (i.e., in contrast to the single virtual node model).
- We also investigate alternative methods to determine the virtual node connections (e.g., randomization in clustering) and compare to the original model with a single virtual node.
- We _theoretically investigate the benefit of using (multiple) virtual nodes_ in terms of two aspects: influence score and the expressiveness in learning a structural link representation.

- We conducted extensive experiments over challenging datasets of different type, provide ablation studies that confirm the superiority of our proposed techniques, analyze the results in detail, and _provide first guidelines about how to use virtual nodes with different types of data and GNNs_ .

- Most importantly, we show that _our virtual node extensions most often yield rather stable performance increases_ and allow standard GNNs to compete with complex state-of-the-art models that also try to improve message passing, as well as with the models leading the OGB leaderboards.

## 2 RELATED WORK

We give an overview on approaches that are similar from a technical perspective; for a more detailed summary, see Appendix A. For a more general overview of the large and diverse field of link prediction, we refer to good summaries in recent works (Martínez et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020).

**Deeper GNNs.** Several techniques address over-smoothing and hence allow for constructing deeper GNNs to solve under-reaching. These models range from the simple but efficient message propagation in SGC (Wu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020) and APPNP (Klicpera et al., 2019a) and connections in JKNet (Xu et al., 2018), to more advanced proposals (Chen et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a) such as the differentiable aggregation functions in DeeperGCN (Li et al., 2020a). However, although (Chen et al., 2020) show that over-smoothing happens particularly in dense graphs, the experiments in most of these works consider citation or recommendation networks, but not the especially dense and important biomedical ones. And our experiments over the latter suggest that the reported results are not generalizable to the more challenging biomedical data.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**Beyond One-Hop Neighbors.** Recently, graph diffusion methods are used in various ways to determine the message targets and thus extend standard message passing beyond the one-hop neighborhood. Atwood & Towsley (2016) use k-hop random walks to extend the node features. APPNP (Klicpera et al., 2019a) applies personalized PageRank to propagate the node predictions generated by a neural network. Other models concatenate (Abu-El-Haija et al., 2019) or aggregate (Xu et al., 2019a; Ma et al., 2020) node embeddings in every layer using a diffusion-based transition matrix. The diffusion-based graph neural network (GDC) (Klicpera et al., 2019b) aggregates information from multiple neighborhood hops at each layer by sparsifying a generalized form of graph diffusion. Subsequent works use diffusion methods on multiple scales (Liao et al., 2019; Luan et al., 2019; Xhonneux et al., 2020) and attention (Wang et al., 2020). Morris et al. (2019) take higher-order graph structures at multiple scales into account during message passing based on the _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler and Leman graph algorithm. All the above approaches are relatively complex, many terminated with memory errors in our experiments, and few have been evaluated for link prediction.

**Virtual Nodes.** To the best of our knowledge, virtual nodes have only been considered in the context of graph classification so far, where a single virtual node (also called _supernode_ ) is added to the graph to be classified and connected to all graph nodes (Gilmer et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Pham et al., 2017; Ishiguro et al., 2019). Note that the original idea was to compute a graph embedding in parallel with the node embeddings and even connected the virtual node only in one direction (i.e, via edges from the graph nodes) instead of bidirectionally (Li et al., 2017).

There are some GNNs which point out special nodes that we could consider as “virtual”. Fey et al. (2020) propose a GNN for molecule graph classification which clusters certain nodes within a molecule using a structure-based, molecule-specific algorithm and then applies message passing within and between these clusters. The graph-partition based message passing from Liao et al. (2018) also used clustering, but it just divides the original messages into inter- and intra-cluster. Our approach creates new “paths” in the graph and we theoretically demonstrate its expressiveness. P-GNN (You et al., 2019) assigns nodes to random clusters (“anchor-sets”) and then creates a message for each node for every anchor-set, while ignoring the message passing from original direct neighbors. Our virtual nodes represent an alternative means to aggregate messages from multiple graph nodes which are not necessarily direct neighbors. We also explore the idea of similar random assignments in our context, but show that more elaborate techniques generally work better. Most importantly, we do not propose a specific, new GNN but a _new technique for augmenting existing graph neural networks_ .

Although it is a well-known trick, the advantage of using virtual nodes has never been theoretically investigated nor fully understood. We focus on link prediction and considerably extend the virtual node technique. There are commonalities in the advantages of using virtual nodes for graph classification and link prediction, but their role in link prediction is to improve the representation of the link instead of the graph (nodes). We analyze theoretically and empirically how they improve GNN performance.

## 3 PRELIMINARIES

**Link Prediction.** We consider an undirected _graph G_ = ( _V, E_ ) with _nodes V_ and _edges E ⊆ V × V_ . Note that this basic choice is only for ease of presentation. All our techniques work for directed graphs and, with simple adaptation, also for graphs with labelled edges. We assume _V_ to be ordered and may refer to a node by its index in _V_ . For a node _v ∈ V_ , _Nv_ denotes the set of its neighbors. Given two nodes, the _link prediction task_ is to predict whether there is a link between them.

**Message-Passing Graph Neural Networks.** In this paper, we usually use the term _graph neural networks_ (GNNs) to denote GNNs that use message passing as described by Gilmer et al. (2017). These networks compute for every _v ∈ V_ a node representation _h[ℓ] v_[at][layer] _[ℓ][∈]_[[1] _[,]_[ 2] _[, . . . , k]_[]][,][by] _aggregating_ its neighbor nodes based on a generic aggregation function and then _combine_ the obtained vector with _h[ℓ] v[−]_[1] as below; _h_[0] _v_[are the initial node features.]

Link prediction with GNNs is usually done by combining (e.g., concatenating) the final representations _h[L] u[, h][L] v[,]_[ of the nodes] _[ u, v]_[ under consideration and passing them through several feed-forward layers] with a final sigmoid function for scoring. We follow this approach.

We further use [1 _, n_ ] to denote an interval [1,2,..., n].

## 4 VIRTUAL NODES IN GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR LINK PREDICTION

So far, virtual nodes have been only used for graph classification. Link prediction scenarios are different in that the graphs are usually very large, heterogeneous, sometimes dense, and the task is to predict a relationship that might strongly be influenced depending on surrounding relations. In the following, we propose approaches that fit these scenarios.

## 4.1 MULTIPLE VIRTUAL NODES

Our main goal of using virtual nodes is to provide a shortcut for sharing information between the graph nodes. However, the amount of information in a graph with possibly millions of nodes is enormous, and likely too much to be captured in a single virtual node embedding. Further, not all information is equally relevant to all nodes. Therefore we suggest to use _multiple virtual nodes S_ = _{s_ 1 _, s_ 2 _. . . , sn}_[1] each being connected to a subset of graph nodes, as determined by an assignment _σ_ : _V →_ [1 _, n_ ]; _n_ is considered as hyperparameter. We propose different methods to obtain _σ_ :

**Random (GNN-RM).** Most simple, we can determine a fixed _σ_ randomly once with initialization.

**Increased Randomness (GNN-RM** _[F]_ **).** Similarly a random assignment, but initialized with every forward pass. In this way, a single bad assignment does not determine the overall performance.

**Clustering (GNN-CM).** Many types of graph data incorporate a certain cluster structure (e.g., collaboration or social networks) that reflects which nodes belong closely together. We propose to connect such nodes in a cluster to a common virtual node, such that the structure inherent to the given graph is reflected in our virtual node assignment _σ_ . More precisely, during initialization, we use a generic clustering algorithm which, given a number _m_ , creates a set _C_ = _{C_ 1 _, C_ 2 _. . . , Cm}_ of clusters (i.e., sets of graph nodes) by computing an assignment _ρ_ : _V →_ [1 _, m_ ], assigning each graph node to a cluster. We then obtain _σ_ by choosing _m_ = _n_ and _σ_ = _ρ_ .

In this work, we decided for the METIS clustering (Karypis & Kumar, 1998) which turned out to provide a good trade off between quality and efficiency. Nevertheless, our idea is generic and can be applied with arbitrary algorithms. We will show ablation experiments for alternatives (e.g., Graclus (Dhillon et al., 2007) and Diffpool (Ying et al., 2018b)).

**Advanced Clustering (GNN-CM**[+] **).** Not every type of graph data contains an inherent cluster structure or one that is sufficiently expressed. Furthermore, using a fixed clustering, we obtain a deterministic algorithm again taking the risk that we completely rely on a single, possibly not ideal, virtual node assignment – there may be critical long range dependencies that go beyond clusters. For these cases, we propose an alternative approach, which breaks up the determinism by extending the above clustering as follows. We choose a relatively large _m_ , with _m ≫ n_ , and apply the above clustering algorithm, during initialization. Then, in each epoch, we randomly guess an assignment _σ[′]_ : [1 _, m_ ] _→_ [1 _, n_ ] of clusters to virtual nodes and define _σ_ ( _n_ ) := _σ[′]_ ( _ρ_ ( _n_ )). Note that this approach is inspired by Chiang et al. (2019), who apply a similar technique is to create batches based on clusters. Further note that we determine _σ[′]_ with every epoch instead of every forward pass since the the computation takes quite some time on large datasets and we observed that this yields good results.

## 5 EVALUATION

We conducted extensive experiments and ablation studies to empirically investigate:

- How does the existing approach with **one virtual node perform in link prediction** ?

- **Do multiple virtual nodes improve performance** , how do our proposed approaches compare?

- In particular, **are approaches based on the graph structure better** ?

- How exactly do virtual nodes support link prediction? **When do they help particularly?**

**Datasets.** We focused on challenging data from the OGB: `ddi` , a drug-drug interaction network; `ppa10` , a subset of the protein-protein association network `ppa` containing only 10% of the train edges (but full valid/test); and `collab` , an author collaboration network. To learn more about smaller data of similar type, we also tested on the citation networks `pubmed` (Yang et al., 2016). Since the datasets are not only very different in type but also in various other critical graph parameters and this is reflected in the performance of the models, we show relevant statistics in Table 1.[3] The datasets vary strongly in size with `ddi` being smallest among the biomedical; on the other hand, `ddi` is very dense. The clustering coefficient intuitively reflects the “cliquishness” of the graph’s subgraphs. The large diameters suggest that the data suits testing under-reaching. Appendix C gives further details and describes datsets we consider in additional experiments in the appendix.
**Baselines** For a competitive comparison, we considered important baselines (described in Section 2):

- The deep GNNs **SGC** , **APPNP** , **DeeperGCN** , and two variants of **JKNet** .

- Approaches extending message passing: **P-GNN** , **APPNP** , **GCN-GDC** , **SAGE-GDC** , **GIN-GDC** .
- The popular GNNs **GCN** (Kipf & Welling, 2017), **SAGE** (Hamilton et al., 2017), and **GIN** (Xu et al., 2019b), which we then extend with (multiple) virtual nodes.

## 5.1 RESULTS

**Overall Impact of Virtual Nodes, Tables 2, 7, 8 (Appendix).** We compare to GCN, SAGE, and GIN. The common approach of using a single virtual node (GNN-VN) yields good improvements over `ddi` , slight improvements over `collab` , but no definitive ones over `ppa10` ; over `pubmed` , it works very well for GIN. The numbers for GNN-RM and GNN-RM _[F]_ reflect the randomness of their connections to the virtual nodes, there is no clear trend. Nevertheless, they clearly outperform the original models, with only few exceptions. The increased randomness by re-assigning the virtual nodes with every forward pass (GNN-RM _[F]_ ) seemingly suits SAGE but not the others. As expected, over the small `pubmed` / `cora` , which also have no cluster structure, the results are not consistent or convincing overall; virtual nodes only yield improvement sometimes, and none for GCN. Yet, on the more challenging datasets, _multiple virtual nodes turn out to be an efficient means to boost the link prediction performance of GNNs_ if they are applied correctly. Our virtual node connections based on the graph structure (GNN-CM) yield consistently good improvements over `ddi` and `collab` , and mostly help on the challenging `ppa10` dataset. On `collab` , we did further experiments using GAT (Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2017) and also observe a clear performance gain: 0.4745 vs. 0.5876 (GAT-CM). GNN-CM and GNN-CM[+] are not always the best ones, but yield reliably good results, in contrast to the other models with virtual nodes (see variability of gray shades). Interestingly, the advanced clustering yields especially good performance over `ppa10` / `ppa` , while its results on the other datasets are not convincing. Generally, the improvements of the virtual node models are strongest on `ddi` . For an in-depth result analysis see Section 5.2.

**Comparison to Related Works and SOTA, Table 3.** Most deep GNNs as well as the models that use complex message-passing techniques perform disappointing and, overall, much worse than the standard GNNs. We did thorough hyperparameter tuning for these models and it is hard to explain. However, most of the original evaluations focus on node or graph classification and consider very different types of data – often the standard citation networks (Lu & Getoor, 2003) – and, in fact, on `collab` we see the best numbers. For a more detailed discussion of P-GNN see Appendix H. Even if we assume that these numbers can be improved, the models do not seem apt for link prediction; in particular, the complex ones: many do not run at all on realistic link prediction data but yield memory errors. Further, _our virtual node extensions make standard GNNs competitive to the models on the leaderboard_ . In particular, their performance is much more stable. The results of the best models from the leaderboard vary strongly with the different datasets, or have not been reported at all. None of these models can be called “good” overall, given the numbers in the - sometimes even missing - rest of the table; in fact, SEAL and Adamic Adar perform rather bad on the very dense `ddi` .

**Impact of Virtual Nodes on Number of GNN Layers and Efficiency, Figure 2.** For the virtual nodes models, the scores increase with the number of layers for a longer time, GCN drops earlier. On `ddi` , GCN-VN and -CM reach their best scores at 6 and 8 layers, respectively, which is remarkable for that very dense dataset, which is prone to over-smoothing. On `collab` it is the other way around. The figure also gives an idea about the runtime increase with using virtual nodes. It compares the 6-layer models, and shows the 4-layer GCN-CM which obtains performance similar to the 6-layer GCN-VN.

**Impact of Virtual Node Number, Figure 3.** First, consider the configurations of the best models for the overall results in Table 2, which are provided in Table 6 in the appendix. Here, we see that the chosen numbers of virtual nodes are indeed random for the “random” models, but GNN-CM consistently uses a high number of virtual nodes, which also suits it better according to our theoretical analysis in Section 4.3. In line with this, the more detailed analysis varying the numbers of virtual nodes, yields best results (also in terms of standard deviations) for SAGE-CM at rather high values. For GCN, we do not see a clear trend, but (second) best performance with 64 virtual nodes. Note that there is a trade off between number of virtual nodes and intra-cluster test edges, discussed in Section 5.2.

**Using Virtual Nodes Only at the Last GNN Layer, Table 4.** Alon & Yahav (2021) show that using a fully connected adjacency matrix at the last layer of a standard GNN helps to better capture information over long ranges. We therefore investigated if it is a better architectural choice to use virtual nodes only at the last layer. However, we see that this can lead to extreme performance drops.

**Impact of Clustering Algorithm, Table 9 (Appendix).** Our architecture is generic in the clustering algorithm, and we investigated the effects of varying that. Graclus is similar in nature to METIS in that it also creates partitions based on the adjacency matrix, but it took much longer to run. Diffpool considers the node features and yields improvements for GCN, but does not scale to larger datasets. Over `ddi` , there is no clear winner and, given its efficiency, METIS turns out to be a good solution.

## 5.2 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS

The results show that our approach with multiple virtual nodes based on graph-based clustering yields performance increases for various GNNs and types of data, but there are clear differences.

**Dense Graphs with Medium/High Clustering Coefficient.** Over `ddi` , we see strongest improvements for all virtual-node models. This can be explained by our proposed theory, showing that a very large node degree _m_ increases the impact of the virtual node(s), and thus decreases the negative impact of the (too) many other neighbors (see Equation (6)). Furthermore, the empirical results confirm our proposed theory regarding multiple virtual nodes (see Equation (7)). We see particularly good numbers for GNN-CM, which exploits the clustering inherent in the given graph. GNN-CM[+] , which considers this given clustering only on a lower level, is shown to perform worse than GNN-CM overall. In fact, we computed the percentage of test edges that occur in the “virtual node cluster” (see Table 11 in the appendix) and it shows that the numbers for the advanced clustering are very similar to the random one, meaning the randomly merged smaller clusters break the data’s structure too much. Interestingly, the experiments show that, even with the dense data that is prone to over-smoothing, virtual nodes make the GNNs score higher with more than the standard 2-3 layers; hence virtual nodes seem to alleviate over-smoothing to some extent, an interesting question for future work.

> **Graphs with Large Problem Radius and Low Clustering Coefficient.** Over `ppa10` , all GNNs use an unusually high number of layers, which hints at a large problem radius (e.g., GCN, which performs especially well, uses 7 layers). Given the very low clustering of the data in addition, `ppa10` represents a special challenge. With the multiple virtual nodes, GNN-CM performs again better than GNN-VN. On the other hand, it does not perform much better than the random models on data without cluster structure. This can be explained by its choice of number of virtual nodes, which is consistently high, but then there are less test edges within a virtual node cluster (see appendix Table 11). We hence see here that the positive effect of having many virtual nodes (recall Equation (7)) cancel out the benefits of clustering. Our advanced clustering, which merges some local clustering with randomness, is able to achieve best results with GCN and GIN (with SAGE, all models perform rather bad over `ppa10` ). This can be explained by the fact that it randomly merges some local clusters – with each epoch anew – and hence allows more messages to pass across “virtual node clusters”. We also did some experiments over the very large `ppa` , which is denser than `ppa10` , and see a similar trend.

**Sparse Graphs with Low to High Clustering Coefficient.** We tested on three citation/collaboration networks of different sizes. Note that, over this data, the problem radius is usually assumed to be rather small (Alon & Yahav, 2021), although the graph diameters are large. We investigated virtual nodes to augment link prediction in large and complex graphs; but we also want to provide insight into the behavior on smaller data. Over `pubmed` (similarly on `cora` as shown in the appendix), virtual nodes do not provide any improvement for GCN. For GIN, a single virtual node yields good increases; overall, it usually outperforms the settings with multiple virtual nodes. We hypothesize that this is mainly due to the small graph size and sparsity. In fact, on the larger and denser `collab` , GNN-CM performs very good for all GNNs. The trends in the models’ performance and the corresponding explanations are similar to those for `ddi` but much less expressed, probably due to the much smaller node degrees. Yet, the performance is much more stable, possibly because it is larger and not as dense.
**Conclusions.** We summarize our main findings to provide first guidelines for applying virtual nodes:
- **Small + Sparse Graphs:** A single virtual node is likely to boost performance of GIN, and virtual nodes should help with SAGE, but probably not with GCN.

- **Large + Sparse Graphs** : If there is cluster structure, GNN-CM should yield stable performance increases. If the problem radius is large or there is few cluster structure, GNN-CM[+] is worth a try.

- **Dense Graphs + Clustering:** Multiple virtual nodes (i.e., GNN-CM) likely increase performance.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

We propose a simple but elegant graph neural network extension using multiple virtual nodes that may considerably increase link prediction performance. We also advance research by providing theoretical justifications - the very first about applying virtual nodes at all - and by showing their positive impact in various experiments. Future work includes the design of more advanced and scalable architectures, and it would be interesting to further investigate the huge performance increases on dense graphs.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a compelling and well-motivated approach to addressing the "under-reaching" problem in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for link prediction—a task often neglected in favor of node classification. The clarity of the writing is high; the introduction effectively bridges the gap between existing graph classification techniques and the specific requirements of link prediction on large-scale, heterogeneous datasets. The conceptual shift from a single global virtual node to a set of clustered, locally relevant virtual nodes is both intuitive and scientifically sound, promising a balance between information propagation and noise reduction. However, a significant potential issue lies in the reliance on graph clustering to determine connectivity: the computational overhead and stability of these clustering algorithms on dynamic or massive-scale OGB graphs remain a critical concern. Furthermore, given that the proposed method aims to compete with state-of-the-art leaderboard models, the paper should rigorously justify how the additional memory and time complexity required for multi-virtual node operations compare to the sophisticated attention or propagation mechanisms they claim to outperform. Ensuring that these efficiency trade-offs are clearly communicated in the empirical section will be essential for the paper's overall impact.

comments.title: Inconsistent Variable Indexing in Multiple Virtual Nodes Definition
comments.quote: S = {s_ 1, s_ 2 . . . , sn} ... assignment σ : V → [1, n]; n is considered as hyperparameter.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1, the authors define a set of virtual nodes $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n \}$ with an assignment mapping $\sigma: V \to [1, n]$. Later, in the description of Advanced Clustering (GNN-CM+), the text states: 'We choose a relatively large $m$, with $m \gg n$, and apply the above clustering algorithm... and define $\sigma(n) := \sigma'( \rho(n) )$'. There is an index confusion here: $n$ is clearly defined as the index of the virtual nodes (the size of set $S$), but here it is used as a variable representing an arbitrary node from $V$. This makes the definition of the composite mapping $\sigma$ mathematically ambiguous.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Definition of the Proposed GNN Update
comments.quote: Note that the highlighted adaptation of the standard GNN processing from Equation (1) is only minor – but powerful... [no equation provided]
comments.explanation: Section 4.2 describes an adaptation to the message-passing scheme to include multiple virtual nodes. While the authors state that the change is 'only minor', the actual mathematical update rule (how the virtual node representation $h_{s_i}$ is aggregated into node $h_v$) is not explicitly provided. Given this is a core contribution of the paper, the reader is left to guess whether the virtual node is treated as an additional neighbor in the neighborhood aggregation or as an additive term. This falls under 'underspecified methods'.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Mathematical Derivation for Influence Score
comments.quote:
Compared to the original probability, we get the following impact ratio for using virtual nodes:

ir ≃ (1 + |V|/|R[k]|)
comments.explanation: The text jumps from an expression involving probability $Prw$ to an 'impact ratio' $ir$ without showing the intermediate steps or providing a formal definition for $|R_k|$ (the set of paths of length $k$). While the authors acknowledge this is a heuristic hypothesis in Section 4.3, the notation $Ir \simeq 1 + |V|/|R_k|$ appears suddenly and lacks the necessary context to verify if the approximation holds for general sparse graphs, as the assumption of an 'm-regular graph' is likely insufficient to justify dropping terms in the denominator for real-world heterogeneous graphs.

comments.title: Inconsistency between hyperparameter notation and definition
comments.quote: We then obtain $\sigma$ by choosing $m$ = $n$ and $\sigma$ = $\rho$.
comments.explanation: In the preceding text, the authors define $n$ as the number of virtual nodes and $m$ as the number of clusters generated by the clustering algorithm (METIS). In the line 'We then obtain $\sigma$ by choosing $m$ = $n$ and $\sigma$ = $\rho$', they equate the number of clusters to the number of virtual nodes, effectively making $m$ and $n$ interchangeable. However, in the very next paragraph ('Advanced Clustering (GNN-CM+)'), they explicitly state 'We choose a relatively large $m$, with $m \gg n$'. This creates a contradiction: in GNN-CM, $m$ must equal $n$ for the assignment to map directly, whereas in GNN-CM+, $m$ is a precursor parameter distinct from $n$. The definition of $m$ changes function mid-section, which is confusing.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in impact ratio derivation
comments.quote:

$m [k]{-} [1]$ When $m$ is large enough, $ir \simeq \{1 + \frac{|V |}{|R_k|}\ \}$.
comments.explanation: The notation in the passage contains OCR artifacts ($m [k]{-} [1]$) that obscure the preceding mathematical expression for 'impact ratio'. Furthermore, the approximation $ir \simeq \{1 + \frac{|V |}{|R_k|}\ \}$ is mathematically opaque. Given $R_k$ as the set of paths of length $k$, $|R_k|$ represents the number of paths, not their aggregate probability or weight. Without a definition of how the virtual node's influence is weighted relative to the graph size $|V|$, equating the ratio to a simple division of nodes by paths is unsupported and likely contains a derivation error or a missing term (e.g., probability of hitting the virtual node).

comments.title: Inconsistent summation index notation
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1, the set of virtual nodes is defined as S={s_1, s_2, ..., sn}. While context implies the number of virtual nodes, a clear distinction between the cardinalities |V| and |S| (or using a different letter for the number of virtual nodes) would be standard.

In standard GNN literature (e.g., Xu et al., 2018), the influence score usually measures the derivative of an output or node embedding at $x$ with respect to the input feature at $y$. The provided formula is notationally cluttered; specifically, $x$ appears as the target node in the LHS and as the 'source' or 'affected' node in the partial derivative. While the intent is clear, the current formulation is technically imprecise regarding which node is the receiver of the gradient.

Given the context of $m$-regular graphs, it likely refers to the count of paths, but without explicitly defining $|R_k|$ as the cardinality of the set of paths, the expression is mathematically underspecified. While the intent is clear, the current formulation is technically imprecise regarding which node is the receiver of the gradient.

comments.title: Unexplained notation in Equation (5) approximation
comments.quote: $ir \simeq \{1 + \frac{|V |}{|R_k| } \}$
comments.explanation: The derivation of the impact ratio $ir$ introduces $|R_k|$, which is earlier defined only as the 'set of paths of length $k$'. In the approximation $ir \simeq 1 + \frac{|V|}{|R_k|}$, it is unclear if $|R_k|$ represents the *number* of paths of length $k$ or some other property of the path set. Given the context of $m$-regular graphs, it likely refers to the count of paths, but without explicitly defining $|R_k|$ as the cardinality of the set of paths, the expression is mathematically underspecified. This makes it difficult to verify if the scaling behavior is consistent with the authors' claim that virtual nodes mitigate over-smoothing.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper tackles a well-motivated and practically relevant problem by adapting virtual nodes to link prediction, proposing a multi-virtual-node framework connected via graph clustering to alleviate under-reaching in standard GNNs. The motivation is clearly articulated, and the intuitive rationale for using clustered virtual shortcuts to facilitate long-range information flow without global noise is strong. However, the manuscript currently lacks sufficient technical clarity regarding the core clustering-to-virtual-node connection mechanism, its exact integration into the message-passing pipeline, and the associated computational overhead, which hinders reproducibility and limits the reader's ability to assess scalability. While the empirical claims of stable performance improvements and competitive results on OGB benchmarks are promising, the evaluation would be strengthened by a more systematic comparison against modern long-range/multi-hop baselines, detailed ablations on clustering strategy and hyperparameters, and a clearer breakdown of runtime/memory trade-offs. The theoretical contributions (influence scores and structural expressiveness) are mentioned but feel underdeveloped relative to the empirical claims, and the novelty positioning requires sharper differentiation from prior graph clustering or diffusion-based shortcut methods. Overall, the idea is compelling and has strong practical potential, but the paper needs deeper technical exposition, more rigorous baseline comparisons, and a tighter alignment between theoretical analysis and empirical evidence to meet publication standards.

comments.title: Contradictory phrasing regarding shortcut mechanism
comments.quote: Intuitively, the virtual node provides a shortcut for message passing between nodes along the graph edges.
comments.explanation: The authors intend to explain that virtual nodes facilitate communication by bypassing long or absent paths in the original graph. However, stating that the shortcut works 'along the graph edges' directly contradicts the concept of a shortcut, as standard message passing occurs along edges. The virtual node actually creates new paths that circumvent the original edge topology. I recommend clarifying that the virtual node shortcuts communication by aggregating information across the entire graph (or within clusters) rather than strictly along existing edges.

comments.title: Citation attribution mismatch
comments.quote: P-GNN (You et al., 2019) assigns nodes to random clusters (“anchor-sets”) and then creates a message for each node for every anchor-set, while ignoring the message passing from original direct neighbors.
comments.explanation: You et al. (2019) published 'How Powerful are Graph Neural Networks?' at NeurIPS 2019, which focuses on the theoretical expressiveness of GNNs and the Weisfeiler-Leman test, not on anchor-sets or P-GNN. The method described closely matches Principal Neighbourhood Aggregation (P-GNN) by Wu et al. (2020) or related works on anchor-based aggregation. Citing the wrong paper for a technical contribution in the Related Work section can mislead readers trying to locate the source algorithm. This should be corrected to the appropriate citation.

comments.title: Grammatical inconsistency in plural/singular usage
comments.quote: We conducted extensive experiments over challenging datasets of different type, provide ablation studies that confirm the superiority of our proposed techniques, analyze the results in detail, and provide first guidelines about how to use virtual nodes with different types of data and GNNs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'datasets of different type' uses a singular noun where the context and subsequent clause ('different types of data') clearly indicate a plural is intended. This is a minor typographical/grammar oversight that could slightly disrupt readability but does not affect technical understanding.

comments.title: Minor typographical error in abbreviation
comments.quote: (i.e, via edges from the graph nodes)
comments.explanation: The parenthetical abbreviation 'i.e,' is missing a space after the comma. While trivial, consistent formatting is expected in academic manuscripts. It should read '(i.e., via edges...' to match standard style guides.

comments.title: Preposition misuse in opening claim
comments.quote: Surprisingly, the impact of virtual nodes with other problems is still an open research question.
comments.explanation: The preposition 'with' is awkwardly paired with 'impact' in this context. The standard and intended phrasing is 'impact of virtual nodes on/for other problems'. This is a minor linguistic issue that could slightly obscure the claim if read too literally.

comments.title: Notation collision: variable `n` used for both virtual node count and node index
comments.quote: Therefore we suggest to use _multiple virtual nodes S_ = _{s_ 1 _, s_ 2 _. . . , sn}_[1] each being connected to a subset of graph nodes, as determined by an assignment _σ_ : _V →_ [1 _, n_ ]; _n_ is considered as hyperparameter. ... define _σ_ ( _n_ ) := _σ[′]_ ( _ρ_ ( _n_ )).
comments.explanation: The variable `n` is explicitly defined as the number of virtual nodes (size of S and codomain of σ), but is later reused as the argument to the assignment functions (`σ(n) := σ'(ρ(n))`), where it should represent a graph node $v \in V$. This notation collision clashes with standard graph conventions ($n$ or $|V|$ for node count, $v$ for node variables) and creates ambiguity in the theoretical extension. Changing $n$ to $v$ in this line would resolve the inconsistency.

comments.title: Questionable claim regarding novelty of virtual nodes in link prediction
comments.quote: Surprisingly, the impact of virtual nodes for the link prediction problem has not been investigated yet.
comments.explanation: The authors position their work as the first to investigate virtual nodes for link prediction. While I appreciate the focused treatment of clustering-based virtual nodes and the theoretical analysis of influence scores, the claim of complete novelty overlooks the widespread use of global aggregation tokens (e.g., the `[CLS]` token in Graph Transformers or auxiliary nodes in message-passing transformers) that function identically to virtual nodes in link prediction and graph classification tasks. It is common for the community to use these as standard baselines or architectural components. Qualifying this claim to distinguish their specific clustering mechanism from general global token usage would strengthen the positioning and avoid unnecessary conflict with adjacent literature.

comments.title: Garbled equations and mathematical inconsistency in impact ratio approximation
comments.quote: When m is large enough, ir can be approximated by ir ≃ 1 + _|V ||R[k] |_ . Here, we see that the impact of virtual nodes grows when m increases.
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction has severely corrupted the mathematical derivations in Section 4.3, making the exact algebraic form of the impact ratio unreadable. More critically, the displayed approximation $ir \simeq 1 + \frac{|V|}{|R^{(k)}|}$ mathematically contradicts the authors' stated conclusion. In graphs with average degree $m$, the number of $k$-step paths $|R^{(k)}|$ scales roughly as $m^k$. As $m$ increases, the denominator grows, causing the fraction to shrink and $ir$ to converge to 1, directly opposing the claim that impact grows with $m$. The missing derivation steps and potential typographical inversion in the formula undermine the theoretical justification for the method's scalability to dense graphs.

comments.title: Inconsistency between text claims and Table 2 results for GNN-CM+
comments.quote: GNN-CM[+] , which considers this given clustering only on a lower level, is shown to perform worse than GNN-CM overall.
comments.explanation: The text asserts that GNN-CM+ performs worse than GNN-CM 'overall'. However, Table 2 explicitly contradicts this for the ddi dataset with the SAGE model: GNN-CM achieves Hits@20 of 0.7564 \u00b1 0.1055, while GNN-CM+ achieves 0.8251 \u00b1 0.0678 (bolded as the best result). Similarly, for GIN on ddi, CM+ (0.5664) outperforms CM (0.5310). While CM+ underperforms CM for GCN on ddi, labeling it as 'worse overall' ignores these direct empirical wins. The authors later acknowledge GNN-CM+ achieves best results on ppa10 for GCN and GIN, making the 'worse overall' phrasing an overgeneralization that conflicts with their own reported numbers.

comments.title: Overstatement regarding 'extreme performance drops' for last-layer-only virtual nodes
comments.quote: We therefore investigated if it is a better architectural choice to use virtual nodes only at the last layer. However, we see that this can lead to extreme performance drops.
comments.explanation: The text characterizes the results of using virtual nodes only at the last layer (VN_OL / CM_OL in Table 4) as leading to 'extreme performance drops'. This characterization is inaccurate for the GCN model on ddi: the baseline (w/o VN) scores 0.5062 \u00b1 0.2186, while VN_OL scores 0.6180 \u00b1 0.0088 and CM_OL scores 0.6322 \u00b1 0.1565. These represent substantial improvements over both the baseline and the full-layer versions. While SAGE and GIN do show notable drops with CM_OL and VN_OL respectively, the claim of 'extreme drops' across the board is contradicted by the GCN results, which show consistent gains. The conclusion should be model-specific rather than universal.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency between derived approximation and stated trend
comments.quote: When _m_ is large enough, _ir_ can be approximated by _ir ≃_ 1 + _|V ||R[k] |_ . Here, we see that the impact of virtual nodes grows when _m_ increases.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the impact ratio $ir$ increases as the average degree $m$ (graph density) increases. However, the provided approximation $ir \simeq 1 + \frac{|V|}{|R^{(k)}|}$ mathematically implies the opposite. In an $m$-regular graph, the number of $k$-step paths $|R^{(k)}|$ scales roughly as $|V| \cdot m^k$. As $m$ grows, the denominator $|R^{(k)}|$ increases exponentially, causing the fraction $\frac{|V|}{|R^{(k)}|}$ to shrink toward zero, meaning $ir$ would converge to 1 (diminishing impact). This directly contradicts the authors' stated conclusion and their empirical observation that virtual nodes yield the strongest improvements on dense biomedical graphs like `ddi`. The approximation likely contains a typographical or derivation error (e.g., perhaps intended as $1 + \frac{m^k}{|V|}$ or a different functional form that increases with $m$), but as written, it undermines the theoretical justification for the method's scalability to dense graphs.

comments.quote:
By adding a virtual node s in one GNN layer, the probability changes to:
Compared to the original probability, we get the following impact ratio for using virtual nodes:

While the authors' intuition (virtual nodes create shortcuts that increase path counts) is clear, the absence of formal expressions makes it impossible to verify the derivations or reproduce the impact ratio. This significantly weakens the theoretical justification of the section.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling conceptual addition to the link prediction literature by proposing a lightweight mechanism—multiple virtual nodes connected via graph clustering—to alleviate the "under-reaching" problem in large, dense graphs. The introduction is clear and effectively articulates the limitations of existing diffusion-based and deep GNN approaches on Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) datasets, positioning the authors' work as a robust alternative that avoids the memory overhead and over-smoothing issues of complex state-of-the-art methods. While the writing is of high quality and the motivation is sound, the most significant issue awaiting empirical validation is the distinctiveness of the proposed clustering strategy; it remains to be seen whether simply introducing multiple virtual nodes provides sufficient benefit over connecting all nodes to a single virtual node, and how the specific clustering algorithm interacts with varying graph densities to guarantee the "stable performance increases" promised in the abstract.

comments.title: Notation conflict: variable `n` reused for size parameter and function argument
comments.quote: We propose different methods to obtain σ: ... n is considered as hyperparameter. ... define σ(n) := σ′(ρ(n)).
comments.explanation: In the context of defining the multiple virtual nodes, the authors use the symbol 'n' to denote the number of virtual nodes and the size of the range for the assignment function σ (i.e., σ : V → [1, n]). Later, in the definition of the advanced clustering algorithm, the authors use 'n' as the argument variable for the function σ (i.e., σ(n) := ...). While the context clarifies that 'n' here refers to a generic node variable due to the subsequent reference to ρ(n), this notation collision is confusing. In standard mathematical writing, it is convention to use a distinct symbol (e.g., v, u, x) for a variable argument to distinguish it from constant parameters or set sizes. This could lead to misinterpretation by a reader glancing at the equation.

comments.title: Inconsistent graph statistics in Table 1
comments.quote: Table 1: Data... Average Node Deg... 500.5 (ddi) ... 8.2 (collab)
comments.explanation: The Average Node Degree values provided in Table 1 (500.5 for `ddi` and 8.2 for `collab`) are mathematically inconsistent with the Node and Edge counts listed in the same table. The paper explicitly states 'All graphs are undirected,' for which the standard definition of Average Node Degree is 2*Edges/Nodes. Calculating this for the provided values: for `ddi`, 2*1,334,889/4,267 ≈ 626; for `collab`, 2*1,285,465/235,868 ≈ 11. The large discrepancies (~25% and ~30% lower than expected) are likely a calculation or data transcription error by the authors. This compromises the ability of the reader to verify the stated density of the datasets (e.g., describing `ddi` as 'very dense').

comments.title: Bibliographic formatting error
comments.quote: Weighted graph cuts without eigenvectors a multilevel approach.
comments.explanation: The title of the cited paper by Dhillon et al. (2007) is 'Weighted graph cuts without eigenvectors: a multilevel approach.' The colon separator is missing, which makes the title appear grammatically incorrect and reduces the utility of the citation for search purposes. While the authors' bibliography is a secondary artifact of the paper, this error is a clear typo that should be corrected.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: Based on the beginning of this paper, the work addresses a timely and practical problem—enhancing link prediction in large, dense graphs by adapting the virtual node concept from graph classification. The writing is clear and well-motivated, with a logical progression from the under-reaching problem to the proposed multiple-virtual-node solution using clustering. The contributions (theoretical analysis, extensive experiments on OGB, comparison with state-of-the-art) are promising. However, several significant issues emerge. First, the novelty is questionable: using multiple virtual nodes with clustering for message passing is reminiscent of existing hierarchical or pooling approaches (e.g., DiffPool, MinCutPool), and the paper does not clearly differentiate itself from such methods in the introduction or related work. Second, the theoretical analysis mentioned in the contributions is not yet visible in the provided excerpt, and its depth and relevance remain unclear. Third, the claim that virtual nodes yield "stable performance increases" and allow "standard GNNs to compete with state-of-the-art" is too broad without seeing the actual results or baselines; the paper risks overpromising. Finally, the related work section is somewhat thin—it focuses on deeper GNNs but does not discuss existing virtual node variants or clustering-based message passing in link prediction, which weakens the contextualization. Overall, the paper has solid potential but needs stronger differentiation from prior work, more concrete theoretical grounding, and careful moderation of its claims.

comments.title: Notation overload for 'n' in GNN-CM+ definition
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper, 'n' is introduced as the number of virtual nodes (S = {s_1, ..., s_n}) and used as a hyperparameter. In this definition, 'n' inside the parentheses is intended to be a variable representing a graph node (like v), but it is written with the same letter as the hyperparameter. This causes a notation conflict: a reader cannot tell whether 'σ(n)' means the assignment for the node number n or the assignment for the hyperparameter value. The surrounding text says 'σ: V → [1,n]' and 'ρ: V → [1,m]', so the composition should be σ(v) = σ'(ρ(v)). Using 'n' as the dummy variable invites confusion with the hyperparameter. The authors likely intended a different letter (e.g., 'v' or 'x'), and the OCR may have misread it, but even with the most plausible intended symbol, the reuse of the same letter is problematic because the hyperparameter n appears in the same sentence ('choose a relatively large m, with m ≫ n'). A careful reader could reasonably misinterpret which n is meant.

comments.explanation: The derivation of the impact ratio is presented in a garbled form due to OCR, making it impossible to verify the mathematical steps. The final approximation 'ir ≃ 1 + |V|/|R^k|' appears to be independent of the node degree m, yet the text claims that 'the impact of virtual nodes grows when m increases'. If |R^k| itself depends on m (as it would in an m-regular graph, where the number of length-k paths is m^k times a combinatorial factor), then the approximation might still depend on m, but the expression as written does not show that dependence. The authors provide no justification for how the approximation is derived or how it implies monotonic growth in m. This conflates a key analytical claim with an insufficiently explained derivation. Even assuming the OCR has mangled the exact formula, the logical gap remains without the full derivation.

comments.title: Overstatement about 'large diameters' across datasets
comments.quote: The large diameters suggest that the data suits testing under-reaching.
comments.explanation: The text claims that the diameters are 'large', but Table 1 shows that `ddi` has a graph diameter of only 5, which is relatively small compared to the other datasets (20–29). While diameter 5 is still larger than typical GNN layer counts (2–3), the unqualified phrase 'large diameters' could mislead a reader into believing that all datasets have large diameters. The authors' intent is to justify that under-reaching is a concern, which is reasonable for all datasets, but the wording overstates the case for `ddi`. The surrounding context does not resolve this, as the sentence immediately follows a reference to Table 1 where the disparity is visible. The claim is not technically false but is imprecise.

comments.title: Inconsistent baseline scores for GCN on `ddi` between Table 2 and Table 4
comments.quote: Table 4: Comparison of using the virtual nodes at every and only at the last layer; Hits@20, `ddi`. ... w/o virtual nodes ... GCN 0.5062_±_0.2186
comments.explanation: In Table 2, the baseline GCN without virtual nodes on `ddi` is reported as 0.4076±0.1073. In Table 4, the same baseline for GCN on `ddi` is given as 0.5062±0.2186, a difference of roughly 0.1. Both tables use the same metric (Hits@20) and the same dataset. The paper does not explain why the baseline differs (e.g., different number of layers, hidden dimensions, or random seeds). Since the ablation in Table 4 is specifically about the placement of virtual nodes, a reader would expect the no-virtual-node baseline to be consistent with the main results table unless explicitly noted otherwise. This inconsistency undermines the reader’s ability to compare the effect of the modification across tables and casts doubt on whether the same underlying GCN configuration was used.

comments.title: Claim that virtual nodes help SAGE on small sparse graphs not supported by presented data
comments.quote: Small + Sparse Graphs: A single virtual node is likely to boost performance of GIN, and virtual nodes should help with SAGE, but probably not with GCN.
comments.explanation: The guideline claims that virtual nodes 'should help with SAGE' on small, sparse graphs. However, the only small, sparse graph shown in the passage is `pubmed` (19,717 nodes, avg. deg. 4.5). In Table 2, SAGE on `pubmed` without virtual nodes achieves 0.9779±0.0105, while SAGE with a single virtual node (VN) achieves 0.9659±0.0333 – a clear decrease. The paper references further experiments on `cora` in the appendix, but the data from the main text does not support the claim. Without additional evidence, this statement may mislead readers about the expected benefit on small, sparse graphs.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: Based on the beginning of this paper, the work addresses a timely and practical problem—enhancing link prediction in large, dense graphs by adapting the virtual node concept from graph classification. The writing is clear and well-motivated, with a logical progression from the under-reaching problem to the proposed multiple-virtual-node solution using clustering. The contributions (theoretical analysis, extensive experiments on OGB, comparison with state-of-the-art) are promising. However, several significant issues emerge. First, the novelty is questionable: using multiple virtual nodes with clustering for message passing is reminiscent of existing hierarchical or pooling approaches (e.g., DiffPool, MinCutPool), and the paper does not clearly differentiate itself from such methods in the introduction or related work. Second, the theoretical analysis mentioned in the contributions is not yet visible in the provided excerpt, and its depth and relevance remain unclear. Third, the claim that virtual nodes yield "stable performance increases" and allow "standard GNNs to compete with state-of-the-art" is too broad without seeing the actual results or baselines; the paper risks overpromising. Finally, the related work section is somewhat thin—it focuses on deeper GNNs but does not discuss existing virtual node variants or clustering-based message passing in link prediction, which weakens the contextualization. Overall, the paper has solid potential but needs stronger differentiation from prior work, more concrete theoretical grounding, and careful moderation of its claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous variable name in definition of assignment for advanced clustering
comments.quote: Then, in each epoch, we randomly guess an assignment σ′ : [1, m] → [1, n] of clusters to virtual nodes and define σ(n) := σ′(ρ(n)).
comments.explanation: In this sentence, the authors use the symbol n as the argument of σ and ρ. However, in the same paragraph n was introduced as the number of virtual nodes (the hyperparameter). Earlier they wrote: 'we suggest to use multiple virtual nodes S = {s1, s2 . . . , sn}[1] each ... as determined by an assignment σ : V → [1, n]; n is considered as hyperparameter.' Using n again as a dummy variable for a vertex creates a notation conflict. A careful reader may be confused whether σ(n) refers to the assignment of the node whose index is the integer n, or whether n is a placeholder for any node (like v). The intended meaning is almost certainly σ(v) for a node v, and the context (via ρ(n)) suggests that n is meant to stand for a node. The inconsistency is easily resolvable but still violates the standard of using distinct symbols for distinct quantities (number of virtual nodes vs. a node variable).

comments.title: Notation overload for 'n' in GNN-CM+ definition
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper, 'n' is introduced as the number of virtual nodes (S = {s_1, ..., s_n}) and used as a hyperparameter. In this definition, 'n' inside the parentheses is intended to be a variable representing a graph node (like v), but it is written with the same letter as the hyperparameter. This causes a notation conflict: a reader cannot tell whether 'σ(n)' means the assignment for the node number n or the assignment for the hyperparameter value. The surrounding text says 'σ: V → [1,n]' and 'ρ: V → [1,m]', so the composition should be σ(v) = σ'(ρ(v)). Using 'n' as the dummy variable invites confusion with the hyperparameter. The authors likely intended a different letter (e.g., 'v' or 'x'), and the OCR may have misread it, but even with the most plausible intended symbol, the reuse of the same letter is problematic because the hyperparameter n appears in the same sentence ('choose a relatively large m, with m ≫ n'). A careful reader could reasonably misinterpret which n is meant.
